sources.title: FDA – Cannabis/CBD in Pregnancy and Breastfeeding
FDA – Warning About THC-Containing Food Products for Children
FDA/FTC – Warning About Delta-8 Copycat Products

items.myth: Cannabis is not addictive.
items.fact: Cannabis can be addictive. The risk increases with frequent use and early initiation.
items.tip: Track your use days and set fixed break days each week.

items.myth: There is no withdrawal from cannabis.
items.fact: When reducing or stopping use, symptoms such as sleep problems, irritability, and restlessness can occur.
items.tip: Lower the dose gradually and actively plan good sleep hygiene.

items.myth: Edibles work immediately, and redosing is harmless.
items.fact: The effect can start much later than expected. Redosing too early increases the risk of overconsumption.
items.tip: After edibles, wait at least 2 hours before taking more.

items.myth: When I use cannabis, I drive more slowly and therefore more safely.
items.fact: Cannabis can impair reaction time, attention, and coordination. This increases accident risk.
items.tip: Keep use and driving strictly separate.

items.myth: If something is legal, it is automatically harmless.
items.fact: Legality says nothing about personal health risk or safer use.
items.tip: Before use, check potency, situation, pre-existing conditions, and medication.

items.myth: Vaping cannabis is completely risk-free.
items.fact: Vaping is not risk-free either. Serious lung injuries associated with cannabis vapes have been reported.
items.tip: If you use, critically review the product source and ingredients.

items.myth: Cannabis smoke is much cleaner than tobacco smoke.
items.fact: Cannabis smoke also contains toxic and carcinogenic substances.
items.tip: Smoke-free alternatives and lower frequency reduce the burden.

items.myth: Secondhand cannabis smoke is harmless.
items.fact: Secondhand exposure can be relevant to health. Indoors, THC can be measurable in the air.
items.tip: Do not use in enclosed spaces when other people are present.

items.myth: THC strength does not matter, as long as the strain fits.
items.fact: Higher THC potency is associated with more side effects and a higher risk of problematic use.
items.tip: With higher THC, choose smaller doses and longer intervals.

items.myth: Adolescents process cannabis the same way adults do.
items.fact: The adolescent brain is still developing. Early and frequent use is associated with increased risks.
items.tip: Delay initiation as much as possible; if use starts early, actively seek support.

items.myth: Cannabis always helps with anxiety.
items.fact: Cannabis can also trigger anxiety, restlessness, or paranoid symptoms, especially at higher doses.
items.tip: Manage set, setting, and dose carefully; pause use if anxiety reactions occur.

items.myth: Psychosis risk has nothing to do with cannabis.
items.fact: Studies show an association between cannabis use and psychosis risk, especially with early and frequent use.
items.tip: Be especially cautious if there is a family history and keep doses low.

items.myth: Cannabis has little effect on the heart and circulation.
items.fact: Cannabis can affect heart rate and blood pressure; this can be relevant for people at risk.
items.tip: If you have cardiovascular disease, clarify use with a medical professional beforehand.

items.myth: A bong or blunt is much easier on the lungs.
items.fact: Combustion creates irritants and harmful substances, regardless of the specific smoking method.
items.tip: If possible, reduce combustion products and plan breaks.

items.myth: Cannabis is harmless during pregnancy because it is plant-based.
items.fact: The CDC and FDA advise against cannabis use during pregnancy; THC can affect the child's development.
items.tip: If you have nausea or sleep problems during pregnancy, discuss medical alternatives.

items.myth: Hardly any THC reaches the baby during breastfeeding.
items.fact: THC can pass into breast milk and remain detectable for an extended period.
items.tip: Avoid THC-containing products while breastfeeding.

items.myth: CBD is completely free of side effects.
items.fact: CBD can cause side effects and interact with medications.
items.tip: If you take long-term medication, clarify interactions with a doctor or pharmacist.

items.myth: CBD products are always clean and correctly dosed.
items.fact: Product quality and labeling can vary; some products unexpectedly contain THC or contaminants.
items.tip: Use only products with transparent analysis and a reliable source.

items.myth: A little THC edible is not a big deal for children.
items.fact: Accidental THC ingestion in children can cause severe symptoms and require clinical treatment.
items.tip: Store THC products locked away, child-resistant, and separate from food.

items.myth: Delta-8 is mild and therefore automatically safe.
items.fact: Delta-8 is psychoactive, can cause unexpected side effects, and is viewed critically by the FDA.
items.tip: If the product situation is unclear, it is better to avoid it.

items.myth: K2/Spice is basically the same as cannabis.
items.fact: Synthetic cannabinoids differ greatly from natural cannabis and can cause severe, sometimes life-threatening reactions.
items.tip: Consistently avoid synthetic cannabinoids.

items.myth: Weekend-only use cannot become problematic.
items.fact: Repeated weekend use can also lead to tolerance, loss of control, or risky situations.
items.tip: Define rules (amount, occasion, breaks) and reflect regularly.

items.myth: Medical cannabis has no side effects.
items.fact: Even in a medical context, there are side effects, interactions, and clear indication limits.
items.tip: Use therapies only with professional support and documented goals.
